annotations.label: Best Diophantine approximation (first definition)

anchor.section: Best Diophantine approximations of a real number
anchor.snippet: the rational number p / q is a best Diophantine approximation of α if

annotations.note: Grepping Mathlib for `bestApprox`/`best_approx` in a Diophantine-approximation sense returns nothing; only Legendre's converse (`Real.exists_rat_eq_convergent`) and Dirichlet's theorem live in `DiophantineApproximation/Basic.lean`.

annotations.label: Best Diophantine approximation (second definition)

anchor.section: Best Diophantine approximations of a real number
anchor.snippet: For the second definition,

annotations.note: No `bestApprox`-style definition (variant with |qα − p| minimization) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second-definition best approximation implies first

anchor.section: Best Diophantine approximations of a real number
anchor.snippet: A best approximation for the second definition is also a best approximation for the first one, but the converse is not true in general.

annotations.note: Since neither notion of best Diophantine approximation is defined in Mathlib, the implication between them is not formalized either.

annotations.label: Best approximations via continued fractions

anchor.section: Best Diophantine approximations of a real number
anchor.snippet: The theory of continued fractions allows us to compute the best approximations of a real number

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Legendre's converse (`Real.exists_rat_eq_convergent`) — any rational with |ξ − q| < 1/(2q.den²) is a convergent — but does not state the general theorem that convergents enumerate the best approximations.

annotations.label: Best approximations of e

anchor.section: Best Diophantine approximations of a real number
anchor.snippet: the constant e = 2.718281828459045235... has the (regular) continued fraction representation

annotations.note: The continued-fraction expansion of `Real.exp 1` and its list of best approximations are not developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Badly approximable number

anchor.snippet: A badly approximable number is an x for which there is a positive constant c such that for all rational p / q we have

annotations.note: Grep for `badlyApproximable` / `BadlyApproximable` in Mathlib returns no hits; the definition is absent.

annotations.label: Characterization via bounded partial quotients

anchor.snippet: The badly approximable numbers are precisely those with bounded partial quotients

annotations.note: Without a `badlyApproximable` definition, the equivalence with boundedness of the partial quotients of the regular continued fraction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalent characterization via Markov constant

anchor.snippet: Equivalently, a number is badly approximable if and only if its Markov constant is finite or equivalently its simple continued fraction is bounded.

annotations.note: Neither the Markov constant of a real number nor this equivalence is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Approximation of a rational by another rational

anchor.section: Approximation of a rational by other rationals
anchor.snippet: is a positive integer and is thus not lower than 1

annotations.note: The pigeonhole 'nonzero integer ⇒ ≥ 1' step underlies `Rat.finite_rat_abs_sub_lt_one_div_den_sq`, which realises the section's conclusion that a rational has only finitely many good rational approximations.

annotations.label: Liouville's theorem on algebraic numbers

anchor.section: Approximation of algebraic numbers, Liouville's result
anchor.snippet: If x is an irrational algebraic number of degree n over the rational numbers, then there exists a constant c ( x ) > 0 such that

annotations.note: `Liouville.exists_pos_real_of_irrational_root` gives, for irrational α that is a root of any nonzero f ∈ ℤ[X], an A>0 with 1 ≤ (b+1)^{deg f} · |α − a/(b+1)| · A — the essential Liouville inequality (bounding by any witness polynomial's degree rather than the minimal one).

annotations.label: Liouville constant

anchor.section: Approximation of algebraic numbers, Liouville's result
anchor.snippet: This result allowed him to produce the first proven example of a transcendental number, the Liouville constant

annotations.note: `liouvilleNumber m = ∑ 1/m^{i!}` is defined and `transcendental_liouvilleNumber` proves it is transcendental for every integer m ≥ 2, covering Liouville's classical m = 10 (and m = 2) constant.

annotations.label: Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem

anchor.section: Approximation of algebraic numbers, Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem
anchor.snippet: If x is an irrational algebraic number and ε > 0 , then there exists a positive real number c ( x , ε ) such that

annotations.note: The Thue–Siegel–Roth theorem is only mentioned in a docstring comment of `LiouvilleWith.lean` and is not proved in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schmidt's simultaneous approximation theorem

anchor.snippet: If x 1 , ..., x n are algebraic numbers such that 1, x 1 , ..., x n are linearly independent over the rational numbers and ε is any given positive real number, then there are only finitely many rational n -tuples

annotations.note: Schmidt's subspace / simultaneous approximation theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Feldman's effective refinement of Baker's theorem

anchor.snippet: a refinement of Baker's theorem by Feldman provides an effective bound: if x is an algebraic number of degree n over the rational numbers, then there exist effectively computable constants

annotations.note: No results by Baker or Feldman on effective Diophantine approximation appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dirichlet's approximation theorem

anchor.snippet: Dirichlet's approximation theorem , which implies that, for every irrational number α , there are infinitely many fractions

annotations.note: `Real.infinite_rat_abs_sub_lt_one_div_den_sq_of_irrational` states that for irrational ξ the set {q : ℚ | |ξ − q| < 1/q.den²} is infinite, which is exactly the statement quoted; the underlying pigeonhole form is `Real.exists_int_int_abs_mul_sub_le`.

annotations.label: Hurwitz's theorem

anchor.snippet: Adolf Hurwitz (1891)

annotations.note: Grep for `Hurwitz` in Mathlib finds only Hurwitz zeta-related material; the Diophantine Hurwitz bound |α − p/q| < 1/(√5 q²) is absent.

annotations.label: Borel's three-convergents theorem

anchor.snippet: Émile Borel (1903)

annotations.note: The statement that among any three consecutive convergents at least one satisfies |α − p/q| < 1/(√5 q²) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent real numbers

anchor.snippet: are called equivalent

annotations.note: The GL₂(ℤ)-action equivalence of real numbers has no dedicated definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Serret's theorem on equivalent irrationals

anchor.snippet: Two irrational numbers x and y are equivalent if and only if there exist two positive integers h and k such that the regular continued fraction representations of x and y

annotations.note: The characterization of GL₂(ℤ)-equivalent irrationals via eventually-equal continued fraction tails is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hurwitz sharpness via golden ratio

anchor.snippet: there are only a finite number of rational numbers p / q such that

annotations.note: The sharpness statement — for the golden ratio only finitely many p/q satisfy |φ − p/q| < 1/(c q²) once c > √5 — is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Improved Hurwitz bound excluding golden-ratio class

anchor.snippet: which is not equivalent to

annotations.note: The refined constant 1/(√8 q²) for numbers not equivalent to the golden ratio (start of the Lagrange spectrum) is not formalized.

annotations.label: ψ-approximable real number

anchor.section: Khinchin's theorem on metric Diophantine approximation and extensions
anchor.snippet: A real number x (not necessarily algebraic) is called

annotations.note: `wellApproximable`/`addWellApproximable` in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.WellApproximable` defines the limsup set of points within δₙ of order-n rationals in a (semi)normed group — a group-theoretic version of the ψ-approximable set — but there is no direct real-number definition.

annotations.label: Khinchin's theorem

anchor.section: Khinchin's theorem on metric Diophantine approximation and extensions
anchor.snippet: Aleksandr Khinchin proved in 1926 that if the series

annotations.note: Grep finds no Khinchin/Khintchine theorem on metric Diophantine approximation in Mathlib; only Gallagher's ergodic theorem (`AddCircle.addWellApproximable_ae_empty_or_univ`) is present.

annotations.label: Koukoulopoulos–Maynard proof of Duffin–Schaeffer

anchor.section: Khinchin's theorem on metric Diophantine approximation and extensions
anchor.snippet: Duffin & Schaeffer (1941) proved a generalization of Khinchin's result

annotations.note: The Koukoulopoulos–Maynard proof is explicitly noted as not included in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.WellApproximable`.

annotations.label: Jarník–Besicovitch theorem

anchor.snippet: The Jarník-Besicovitch theorem, due to V. Jarník and A. S. Besicovitch , states that the Hausdorff dimension of this set is equal to

annotations.note: The dim_H = 2/(1+β) formula for the set of β-approximable numbers is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jarník's theorem on badly approximable numbers

anchor.snippet: V. Jarník proved that the Hausdorff dimension of this set is equal to one

annotations.note: Since badly approximable numbers are not defined in Mathlib, the statement that their set has full Hausdorff dimension is absent.

annotations.label: Schmidt's incompressibility theorem

anchor.snippet: This result was improved by W. M. Schmidt , who showed that the set of badly approximable numbers is incompressible

annotations.note: Schmidt's winning-set / incompressibility theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weyl's equidistribution criterion

anchor.snippet: Hermann Weyl proved a basic result showing that this was equivalent to bounds for exponential sums formed from the sequence.

annotations.note: No formalization of Weyl's equidistribution criterion (equivalence between equidistribution mod 1 and vanishing exponential sums) is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Very well approximable numbers

anchor.snippet: known as the set of very well approximable numbers

annotations.note: The set of numbers which are ψ-approximable for ψ(q)=q^{-c} for some c>1; stated to have Hausdorff dimension one.

annotations.label: Liouville numbers

anchor.snippet: known as the set of Liouville numbers

annotations.note: Numbers ψ-approximable for ψ(q)=q^{-c} for all c>1; stated here to have Hausdorff dimension zero.

annotations.label: Well approximable number

anchor.snippet: a number is called well approximable if it is not badly approximable

annotations.note: Without a `badlyApproximable` predicate for reals in Mathlib, its complement 'well approximable' (in the sense of the Wikipedia article) is not defined either; the unrelated `wellApproximable` set is about limsups in seminormed groups.

annotations.label: Duffin–Schaeffer conjecture

anchor.section: Khinchin's theorem on metric Diophantine approximation and extensions
anchor.snippet: posed what is now known as the Duffin–Schaeffer conjecture on the analogue of Khinchin's dichotomy for general, not necessarily decreasing, sequences

annotations.note: The original 1941 conjecture, later proved by Koukoulopoulos–Maynard (2019).

annotations.label: Beresnevich–Velani Hausdorff-measure equivalence

anchor.section: Khinchin's theorem on metric Diophantine approximation and extensions
anchor.snippet: Beresnevich & Velani (2006) proved that a Hausdorff measure analogue of the Duffin–Schaeffer conjecture is equivalent to the original Duffin–Schaeffer conjecture

annotations.note: The Beresnevich–Velani mass-transference / Hausdorff-measure equivalence is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schmidt's higher-dimensional generalization of Jarník's theorem

anchor.snippet: Schmidt also generalized Jarník's theorem to higher dimensions

annotations.note: No higher-dimensional Jarník/Schmidt Hausdorff-dimension result appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Littlewood conjecture

anchor.snippet: the Littlewood conjecture

annotations.note: Grep for `Littlewood` in Mathlib returns no results.

annotations.label: Lonely runner conjecture

anchor.snippet: the lonely runner conjecture

annotations.note: Grep for `LonelyRunner` / `lonely_runner` in Mathlib returns no results.

annotations.label: Margulis's proof of the Oppenheim conjecture

anchor.snippet: the proof of the decades-old Oppenheim conjecture by Margulis

annotations.note: Grep for `Oppenheim` in Mathlib returns no results.

annotations.label: Kleinbock–Margulis proof of Baker–Sprindzhuk conjectures

anchor.snippet: the proof of Baker and Sprindzhuk conjectures in the Diophantine approximations on manifolds by Kleinbock and Margulis

annotations.note: Grep for `Sprindzhuk`, `Kleinbock`, or a manifold-Diophantine result finds nothing in Mathlib.
